title: Polydactyl Cats: Hemingway and the Six-Toed Felines 🐱
description: Discover the genetics behind cats with extra toes. Ernest Hemingway had 54 polydactyl cats. What is the dominant gene causing this mutation? Facts about the Hemingway House and its legendary multi-toed cats.

segments.voice: Polydactyl cats are those born with more toes than usual. The word 'polydactyl' comes from the Greek 'poly' (many) and 'daktylos' (toes). While normal cats have 18 toes, polydactyl cats can have up to 28.

Ernest Hemingway, the famous American writer, was a passionate lover of polydactyl cats. It started with a cat gifted by a ship captain in the 1930s, and his collection grew to 54 cats at his home in Key West, Florida.
Hemingway called his polydactyl cats his 'lucky cats' or 'house cats.' Some of their descendants still live at the Hemingway House Museum in Key West, which is now a popular tourist destination.

Polydactyly in cats is caused by a dominant gene. This means they only need to inherit the gene from one parent to express the trait. If a polydactyl cat reproduces, there's a 50% chance its offspring will also be polydactyl.
Polydactyl cats are more common in certain regions. In New England and Canada, about 10-20% of the local cat population has this trait. In other parts of the world, it's much rarer.
A study by Cornell University analyzed genetic records of 1,200 polydactyl cats. They found the trait didn't cause significant health issues in most cases.
However, polydactyl cats can have problems if the extra toes aren't well-developed. In some cases, rudimentary toes may cause discomfort or infection.
The toe configurations vary widely. Some cats have extra toes that stick out sideways, while others have toes that point upward like giant mittens.
segments.visual: cat paw six toes close up

segments.voice: Polydactyl cats often have better stability and gripping ability. Some believe this gives them an evolutionary advantage for climbing and hunting—though this has never been scientifically proven.
Hemingway claimed his polydactyl cats brought him good luck with his writing. During his time in Key West, he wrote some of his most famous works, including 'For Whom the Bell Tolls.'
The Hemingway House Museum is home to about 60 cats today, many of them direct descendants of Hemingway's original cats. Most remain polydactyl.
In modern domestic cats, an estimated 1-5% of the population is polydactyl. It's more common in mixed-breed cats than purebred ones.
The polydactyl gene has been mapped to feline chromosome 19. Researchers at Uppsala University in Sweden identified the specific responsible gene in 2007.
Some polydactyl cats are so famous they've become tourist attractions. Polydactyl Cat Island in New York is a sanctuary home to 60 cats with this genetic trait.
Polydactyl cats can have extra toes on their front paws, hind paws, or both. The most common configuration is one or two extra toes on the front paws.
A famous polydactyl cat in the 1900s was Clarence, a ship's cat who lived in New York Harbor. Sailors supposedly believed he brought good luck.
If you have a polydactyl cat, it's important to properly care for those extra toes. Their nails should be trimmed carefully to avoid infections or irritation.
In conclusion, polydactyl cats are a remarkable genetic marvel. Thanks to Hemingway and his legacy, these unique cats continue to be cherished and protected worldwide.
